message:
>  Her comments further distanced me from supporting her even if she had some valid points on policy. 

Are you "distanced" from the other side when they say Democrats all (not 1/2...all) hate God and America, or that they should be hunted like rabid coyotes?


>  How is her comments any different than being an "ist" herself? 

Hating intolerance is not intolerant.  Claiming otherwise is simplistic and dishonest.

The problem with calling anyone racist or sexist or "ist" is that the person you call them that KNOWS (or believes in their heart) they are not in fact "ist." This fact negates the comments at a base level and frankly removes the desire to engage with the person who made the comments.

As an example, I am NOT a Trump supporter, but I am also NOT a Clinton Supporter either (I served in the military during the Clinton Administration). Her comments further distanced me from supporting her even if she had some valid points on policy. By her calling (implying) that half (or even a sizable portion of the US) was "ist" she insulted America as a whole. 

How is her comments any different than being an "ist" herself? She is making stereotypical comments based on limited interactions. She is doing exactly what she accused her opponents of being. Whatever point she "might" have had was washed away with the same sin.

@annoying-view @doughy-gun It's important to call out bad behavior, but we need to do it in a way that doesn't alienate people. We can't make progress if we're just shouting past each other. Let's focus on the issues and find common ground.

It’s about finding a balance—promoting positive actions while still engaging in constructive dialogue about the policies that can make a real difference. Let’s keep the conversation respectful and focused on solutions. #StayUnited

@annoying-view That's a pretty narrow view. Just because hobbies aren't direct solutions doesn't mean they don't inspire or relax us to tackle bigger issues later. Plus, awareness often starts with passion projects like these. #BalanceMatters

@annoying-view It's easy to dismiss hobbies, but they play a big role in keeping us engaged and passionate about life. Bird watching has shown me so much beauty in nature, which only strengthens my resolve to protect it. Maybe you should try finding your own way to connect with the world around you. #FindYourPassion

You can't compare other countries to the US. No other country is like the US. It's called the melting pot for a reason, we have a very diverse country. Tons of different, races, religions, beliefs, morals, etc. all mixed into one country. We're bound to have lots of disagreements, some of which lead to arguments, some of which lead to violence. It's not like Sweden where 98% of their population is Christian and white. Everyone has the same or very similar beliefs and are a lot less likely to butt heads. I'm not saying other countries don't have diversity, I'm saying we have a fuck ton more than everyone else, this shit is bound to happen here. And when seconds matter, the police are minutes away. I just like to know that I don't have to rely on anybody else when my front door gets kicked in. Guns don't have a prayer at getting banned here, at least not for a long time. But if they ever do, I'll just get an illegal one like the criminals do, I'll never put myself in a situation where I'm powerless to someone else because a bunch of fucktards voted against me being able to defend myself.

Since you purport to describe fundamental human behavior, it should be the same everywhere, right?

So, why are other countries with stricter gun laws so much safer than the US?

@rigid-mainmast, while I understand the point about diversity, it doesn't justify the level of violence we see. Countries with strict gun laws manage to handle their diversity without the same levels of gun-related deaths. It's not just about having guns for self-defense; it's about creating a society where people feel safe without needing to arm themselves constantly.

@rigid-mainmast, so basically you're saying the US is just too chaotic and diverse to handle anything but guns for safety? Sounds like a cop-out. Other countries manage it, why can't we? Maybe it's time to actually address the root issues instead of just arming to the teeth. #justsaying

@null-culture, @spicy-guitar, you guys really think other countries are handling it better? Have you seen their crime rates when they take away guns? People need to take responsibility for their own safety. It’s not a cop-out, it’s common sense. #StaySafeNotSorry

@rigid-mainmast, common sense, huh? So the solution to everything is just to carry a gun? What happened to addressing the actual problems, like mental health, poverty, and education? Seems like carrying a gun is easier than fixing society. #StaySafeButAlsoAddressTheRoots

@null-culture, addressing those issues is great, but it doesn't change the fact that sometimes you need to protect yourself right now, not years down the line after all the problems are fixed. Carrying a gun is about immediate safety, not ignoring other issues. #RealTalk
@rigid-mainmast, so basically you're saying we should all walk around like superheroes with capes and concealed carry permits? Sounds like a fun world where everyone's a vigilante! #JustSaying

@snow-array, I guess in your world, superheroes are the only ones who can handle real threats. In reality, everyday people just want to feel safe without having to call for Superman every time they step out. #BeReal

@rigid-mainmast, feeling safe shouldn't mean turning everyone into a superhero or a vigilante. Maybe instead of carrying guns, we could focus on actually fixing what makes us feel unsafe in the first place? Just a thought. #PeaceNotPieces

message:
>From a business perspective we are the home and birthplace of Google, Amazon and numerous other cutting edge companies that will kickstart the next generation of technology; also think Tesla, Space-X. We are home to the computers you use every day and probably posted this message on like Apple, Microsoft. We even created this goddamn website - reddit. Which of those companies are driving the collapse of America as we know it? What's the U.K. or European equivalent of any of these businesses? Every drink a Coke, or eat at McDonalds? Ever fly in a Boeing Jet? Ever use Netflix?

I don't judge a country based on its businesses, but by how a country acts. From what I've seen, the American populace is substantially more unhinged than other nations.

> Murder in America is at an all time low if you look at the last 30-60 years.

Since 2007 many of the worst gun massacres in the United States have taken place. Even if the overall murder rate is lower, it's still high enough for constant massacres, and certainly has the highest murder rate in the first world.

> So what exactly is collapsing?

The reputation of the United States. The way the world sees Americans and America. The country just feels a lot darker and uglier these days.

So with respect to comment one, you don't judge a country on what it contributes to the world? That is an odd stance in my opinion. Would you be comfortable throwing away all modern products from American companies at this moment? I know I'd be fine - right at this very moment - throwing away all modern UK made products. Wouldn't be happy about it, but my life would be mostly unchanged as I could still google things and use my laptop, etc. 

Regards to comment two, yes mass murders have increased but they have in other countries like Europe as well. What about the Bataclan in Paris, or the Manchester suicide bombing? Not a happy thing to discuss, but they're problems both continents are facing right now. 

Could you elaborate more?

message:
This is an interesting question and I think there are some good responses but I’ll add a thought or two.   
     
I want to set aside race for a second to get a simpler example. If someone was raped by a man wearing glasses, I think you could expect two things:
One, she would have a visceral, fearful reaction when seeing a man with glasses. 
Two, she would intellectually understand that men wearing glasses are no more likely to assault her than men without glasses.     
     
Their initial gut reaction in any case is valid. If you see someone who looks like your attacker, it is natural to become fearful. How you process that response should be different though. Since it is clear that men with glasses are no more likely to rape someone than men without glasses, hopefully you can get to a point where they are comfortable around men with glasses again. 

As far as avoiding men after a sexual assault, I would say that it is slightly unfair to men who would never assault someone, but it is much more understandable. If I saw someone walking down the street with a gun and they shot me, I will probably avoid people carrying guns in the street. It might be unfair to law-abiding gun owners, but the fact that someone has a gun in public is related to my injury. Similarly, the sex of strangers around a woman is related to the type of crime (sexual assault) whereas race is not. 

Hopefully that was coherent, sorry if it wasn’t.

message:
The major difference between children and adults, as it relates to this problem, is that children are not really able to defend their own interests and beliefs. And in many cases, children don't even have fully formed interests and beliefs until they're older. I understand that there is a bit of a grey area with older teens, who certainly have the capability to think and reason, but younger children and preteens are simply not mentally developed enough to contribute to society in the ways that adults do. Furthermore, children often lack the capability or experience to think of the long term effects or fairness or morality of an idea.

- Not able to vote

I believe this restriction is in place so children don't blindly vote based on what their parents believe, or what is popular at the time, or what is appealing to their still undeveloped brains, etc. 

- Not able to get a job

This is to protect children from unfair work conditions, actually. IIRC, it's still very possible to get a job as a child, you just need to jump through a few more hoops to verify that you're not being forced to work or treated unfairly.

- Going to the bathroom

This one is pretty dumb, but one must consider that if no one was harming the educational system through going to the bathroom, this rule would never have been enacted. The privilege was probably abused at some point in history (it was in high school, for me), so it was taken away.

- If 15/16 year olds were allowed to vote in this past election, who knows? We could have had anyone as our president.

Children don't have the same interests as adults, though (or, the interests of adults simply aren't as important to them yet). I could likely run for president, promising everyone a free cell phone and free college education, and get a majority of the younger generation's votes. Children don't often consider the long term consequences of their actions.

- They have to go to school for 8 hours a day, 180 days of the year

Only up to age 16 in most states, at which point you are able to drop out if you want. 10th grade is the lowest level of education that the government has deemed you need to function in our society. While you may have some philosophical arguments against forcing people to learn, I don't think that the actual aim of compulsory education is evil -- in fact, it's protective and helpful to those who undergo the process.

-Most parents won't let them go out for a walk without their explicit permission

This has entirely to do with the neighborhood and surrounding area. I know many parents in safe areas that let their kids do whatever they want. While your experience may be different, it is not the case for every child.
